title: SIG on Advance Payment of Tax (COVID 19) [Withdrawn]
text:
STANDARD INTERPRETATION GUIDELINE 2020-08
INCOME TAX ACT 2015 – SECTION 110A - ADVANCE PAYMENTS OF TAX DURING COVID-
19 PANDEMIC
This Standard Interpretation Guideline (SIG) sets out Fiji Revenue and Customs Service’s (FRCS) policy and
operational practice in relation to the Loss Carried Forward.
The SIG is issued with the authority of the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of FRCS.
All legislative references in this SIG are to the Income Tax Act 2015 (unless otherwise stated).
This SIG is in effect from 1 April 2020 and may need to be reviewed in the event of any relevant legislative
amendments.
CONTENT
Purpose 2
Introduction 2
Legislative Analysis 2
Appendix 6
1 | P age
A world class revenue service delivering excellence in revenue collection, border protection, trade and travel facilitation

PURPOSE
1. The purpose of this Standard Interpretation Guideline (SIG) is to issue a practical guidance on
amendments made to the Income Tax Act 2015 (ITA) in relation to new rules for the payment of
advance tax in the supplementary COVID-19 Response Budget.
2. The amendments have been effected through Act No. 3 of 2020 which is attached in the Appendix
INTRODUCTION
3. The COVID-19 response supplementary budget brought in new rules for the payment of advance
tax during the corona virus pandemic.
4. The policy intention behind the new rules are reflected in the budget address which is as stated
below:
“The rule for advance payment of tax for companies and other taxpayers will be relaxed from the
current three payments at a rate of 33.3 per cent to nine payments at a rate of 11.1 per cent. This
more flexible and affordable payment schedule will be based on their post-COVID adjusted profits,
offering serious financial relief to businesses, sole traders, and partnerships. Penalties on estimated
tax will also be removed, with this policy lasting through 31 December 2020.”
5. The examples used throughout this SIG are merely illustrative. They do not cover the infinite number
of factual scenarios that may arise. The relevant legislative provisions must be considered and
applied to each case on its particular facts. That is, conclusions should not be drawn by determining
whether the facts of a particulars case may be analogous with particular examples, but rather on
the basis of applying the correct tests established by the law.
6. In this SIG, any reference to the term “Minister” is a reference to the Minister responsible for
Finance.
7. The full text of the legislative provisions is contained in the Appendix.
LEGISLATIVE ANALYSIS
8. The COVID-19 budget amendments have introduced section 110A in the Income Tax Act 2015 which
has new rules for advance payments of tax during coronavirus disease (COVID-19) pandemic. The
new rules provide changes in the dates and frequency of advance payments.
2 | P age
A world class revenue service delivering excellence in revenue collection, border protection, trade and travel facilitation

9. The changes for the dates of advance payments are highlighted below:
Section 110 Section 110A (changed to) Conditions
Company last day of the sixth, last day of the sixth, seventh, a) the 2019 tax year ending on
ninth and twelfth eighth, ninth, tenth, eleventh any date from 30 April 2020 to
months of the tax and twelfth months of the tax 30 June 2020; or
year year and the first and second b) the 2020 tax year ending on
months of the following tax any date from 31 July 2020 to
year 31 December 2020
Any 30 April, 31 August 30 April, 31 May, 30 June, 31
other and 30 November July, 31 August, 30 September, 2020 tax year
person 31 October, 30 November and
31 December
10. Therefore, if a company has a 2019 tax year which ends on any dates between 30 April 2020 to 30
June 2020 and 2020 tax year ending on 31 July 2020 to 31 December 2020, the payment dates
would increase from 3 payments to nine payments. Any other person would make nine payments
instead of three for the 2020 tax year as well.
11. With the increased frequency of payments, a new formula for calculating the advance payment
tax will be used to effect lowered payments. The formula1 that would be used is:
1
11 % ×(𝐴−𝐵)
9
where—
A is the person’s assessed Income Tax liability for the preceding tax year, including under a
self-assessment, after reduction of any foreign tax credit allowed to the person for that
year; and
B is so much of A that was paid by amounts withheld under Subdivision 4 of Division 2.
12. In the event where the preceding income tax liability for the previous year has not been calculated
or the person has commenced to derive income included in gross income during the tax year, an
estimated income tax liability is used to calculate the advance tax payable2.
The advance tax is calculated by the following:
1 estimated income tax liability
𝑜𝑓 estimated income tax liability or
9 9
1 Section 110A(3) of the ITA 2015
2 Section 110A(4) of ITA 2015
3 | P age
A world class revenue service delivering excellence in revenue collection, border protection, trade and travel facilitation

13. Where the income tax liability is estimated, a statement of the estimated income tax to be payable
by the person must be filed with the CEO at any time before the due date of the seventh advance
payment, that is:
a. before the twelfth month of the year for a company; or
b. before 31st October for any other person3.
If the statement is not filed by the person for a tax year, the estimated income tax liability payable
will be estimated by the CEO4.
14. If the person has reasonable grounds to believe that the income tax liability for the year will be
significantly lower that than the income tax liability for the previous year, the person may file a
statement of the anticipated income tax liability.
The statement must be filed any time before the end of the twelfth month of a company’s tax year
or before 31st October 2020 for any other person.
15. The other rules for advance payments such as statement of a revised estimate, and crediting of
advance tax paid against the person’s taxable liability remains the same.
16. Example
Taxpayer ABCo has its tax year as 1st June 2019 to 31st May 2020. In its 2019 tax year ABCo has
already made an advance payment on the 6th and 9th month of its tax year. ABCo had a tax liability
of $90,000 in 2018. The advance payments were made as follows:
30th November 2019 : $30,000
29th February 2020 : $30,000
31st May 2020 : $30,000
The amendments to the Income Tax Act are effective from 1st April 2020. How will ABCo account for
the remaining payment of advance tax?
The remaining payment of $30,000 would be payable on the twelfth month, and first and second
months of the following tax year. This equates to the following payments on the following dates:
31st May 2020 : $10,000
31st June 2020 : $10,000
31st July 2020 : $10,000
3 Section 110A(5) of ITA 2015
4 Section 110A(7) of ITA 2015
4 | P age
A world class revenue service delivering excellence in revenue collection, border protection, trade and travel facilitation

17. For taxpayers, who do not fall in the above category however are impacted by COVID 19 and have
difficulties in making advance tax payments, can make time-to-pay arrangement with the Debt
Management Services under section 25 of the Tax Administration Act 2009.
18. For further information and clarification in regard to this SIG, please email us at tipu@frcs.org.fj
